episodes.title: Barefoot Daily News Bulletin — Wednesday 15 July 2026
episodes.intro: Good morning. It's Wednesday the fifteenth of July, and here is the Barefoot Daily News Bulletin, with today's fishing and outdoors news from the Top End, around Australia and across the wider world of angling.

stories.headline: Hundreds turn out for the Daly River Barra Classic, running since 1982
stories.summary: Hundreds of anglers have gathered on a remote stretch of the Daly River for the week-long Barra Classic, the Northern Territory tournament held on the same river every year since 1982. Australian cricket identity Merv Hughes, who has fished it for close to two decades, described it as the premier fishing competition in the country. Competitors reported a new challenge this year, with sharks taking hooked barramundi before they could be landed.
stories.spoken: Hundreds of anglers have gathered on a remote stretch of the Daly River for the week-long Barra Classic, the Northern Territory competition that has run on the same water every year since nineteen eighty-two. Among them is the former Australian fast bowler Merv Hughes, who has fished the event for close to twenty years and calls it the premier fishing competition in the country. One new hurdle this season: sharks have been taking hooked barramundi before anglers can bring them to the boat.

stories.headline: Pro tournament scoring tech now free for everyday fishing clubs
stories.summary: Fishing tackle brand BUBBA and Major League Fishing have opened up their professional tournament scoring system to everyday anglers. SCORETRACKER LIVE, which has logged more than ninety-one thousand hours powering live leaderboards at top-level bass tournaments, is now available free through the BUBBA app for local clubs, junior events and backyard competitions to run their own real-time scoring. The platform made its public debut this week at the ICAST Cup in Kissimmee, Florida, raising funds for the Keep America Fishing conservation program.
stories.spoken: Recreational fishing clubs can now run their tournaments with the same scoring technology used on the professional circuit. Fishing brand BUBBA has partnered with Major League Fishing to release SCORETRACKER LIVE to the public through its app, free of charge. The system, already used for more than ninety-one thousand hours of live leaderboards at top bass tournaments, lets club organisers, competitors and spectators follow every catch in real time. It made its public debut this week at a tournament in Kissimmee, Florida, raising money for a fish conservation program.

stories.headline: Norwegian hook maker unveils new point design at world's biggest tackle show
stories.summary: Norwegian hook manufacturer Mustad is showcasing new hook-point technology at this week's ICAST tackle show in Orlando, Florida. The DeltaPoint features a precision-forged triangular point with an internal rib running from barb to tip, a design the company says increases strength without adding weight, delivering faster hooksets and greater durability. It debuts on the Mustad 7691S Swordfish Hook, built for offshore swordfishing, tuna and wahoo trolling.
stories.spoken: A new hook-point design from Norwegian manufacturer Mustad is among the technology on show at ICAST, the world's largest fishing tackle trade show, which opens in Orlando, Florida, this week. The DeltaPoint uses a precision-forged, triangular point with an internal rib running its length, reinforcing strength without adding weight. Mustad says the result is a faster hookset and a more durable point, and it will first appear on a heavy-duty hook built for offshore swordfishing and tuna trolling.

stories.headline: Daiwa releases budget-friendly DX rod series
stories.summary: Tackle maker Daiwa has released its new DX series of rods for the Australian market, covering sixteen models from ultralight estuary spin outfits to heavier freshwater and inshore setups. The range uses Daiwa's Braiding X graphite blank construction paired with Fuji guides and reel seats, aimed at anglers wanting mid-range performance at an entry-level price.
stories.spoken: Daiwa has released a new budget-friendly rod range for Australian anglers. The DX series spans sixteen models, from light estuary spin outfits through to heavier freshwater and inshore rods, built around the company's Braiding X graphite blank and fitted with Fuji guides and reel seats. Daiwa says the aim was to bring solid mid-range performance to anglers starting out or fishing on a budget.

stories.headline: Record $3.25 million prize purse announced for new bass fishing championship
stories.summary: A new bass fishing tournament in Nashville, Tennessee will carry the richest prize purse in the sport's history. World Bass Enterprises has confirmed "The Champions," a five-day event in October at Old Hickory Lake, will pay 3.25 million US dollars in total prize money, including 1.25 million to the winning angler, the largest single prize ever awarded in competitive bass fishing. Fifty anglers will compete, including names from Major League Fishing's Bass Pro Tour. To mark the launch, organisers are also giving fans the chance to win the CEO's personal 84,000 dollar custom bass boat.
stories.spoken: Nashville will host the richest prize purse in the history of competitive bass fishing this October. World Bass Enterprises has confirmed its new event, The Champions, will pay out three and a quarter million US dollars in total, including one and a quarter million to the winning angler alone, the biggest single prize the sport has seen. Fifty anglers will take part, among them competitors from Major League Fishing's Bass Pro Tour, in a five-day event on Old Hickory Lake that also includes a free public expo and concerts. To celebrate, organisers are giving fans a chance to win the company founder's own custom bass boat, valued at around eighty-four thousand dollars.

stories.headline: Idaho's South Fork Snake River fishing best at dawn and dusk as heat builds
stories.summary: Fly anglers on Idaho's South Fork Snake River are being advised to fish early and late as a heatwave pushes water temperatures up and air temperatures toward 100 degrees Fahrenheit. With flows running high out of Palisades Dam, guides report the best dry fly fishing is in the first two hours of daylight, before wind picks up, with a short but productive evening caddis rise later on. Through the heat of the day, fish are holding deep and tight to cover, favouring nymphs fished along the river's main channel edges.
stories.spoken: On Idaho's South Fork Snake River, anglers are being told to fish at the edges of the day as a heatwave takes hold. With air temperatures pushing into the high thirties Celsius and strong winds building through the morning, the best dry fly fishing is happening in the first couple of hours after dawn, before conditions turn tough. Fish are moving into shaded banks and deeper water through the middle of the day, and a brief but lively caddis hatch in the evening is providing the day's best remaining window on top.

stories.headline: Seven-year-old lands state-record gar in his pyjamas
stories.summary: A family fishing trip on Minnesota's Minnesota River produced an unexpected state record. Seven-year-old Isaac Richert, fishing in his pyjamas while his father and brother set up camp nearby, hooked what turned out to be a 30.5-inch shortnose gar, the first fish to meet the length threshold for Minnesota's catch-and-release record category since it was introduced in 2024. The fish was measured, photographed and released.
stories.spoken: A family camping trip on the Minnesota River has produced a new state fishing record, and the angler was still in his pyjamas when he caught it. Seven-year-old Isaac Richert was minding the family's baited rods after dark when one bent over hard. What he expected to be a catfish turned out to be a shortnose gar measuring thirty and a half inches, enough to claim Minnesota's catch-and-release record for the species, a category no angler had qualified for since it was introduced two years ago. The fish was measured, photographed and released unharmed.

stories.headline: The bass tournament near-misses that live on in fishing folklore
stories.summary: A look back at some of competitive bass fishing's most agonising near-misses shows just how fine the margins can be. In the 1989 Bassmaster Classic, angler Jim Bitter lost the title by two ounces after a fish he was measuring flopped off his boat and back into the water. Eight years later, Dalton Bobo missed out on the same title by a single ounce after a dead-fish penalty. Writer Shaye Baker, who lost second place at a 2013 tournament over an accidental extra fish in his livewell, says the stories still resonate with anglers decades on.
stories.spoken: Competitive bass fishing has produced some famously narrow misses over the years. At the 1989 Bassmaster Classic, angler Jim Bitter lost the title by just two ounces after a fish slipped from his hands and back into the water while he was measuring it. In 1997, Dalton Bobo missed the same title by a single ounce because of a penalty for a fish that didn't survive in his boat's holding tank. Fishing writer Shaye Baker, reflecting on both, recalls his own costly mistake at a 2013 tournament, when an extra fish accidentally left in his holding tank cost him a place on the podium.

stories.headline: New fish habitat project gives tournament bass a place to recover
stories.summary: A new artificial habitat zone has been installed at Oklahoma's Lake Eufaula to help bass recover after tournament weigh-ins. Major League Fishing's Fisheries Management Division worked with state wildlife authorities and a local equipment dealer to anchor a network of long-lasting habitat structures at the mouth of Eufaula Cove, replacing the cedar trees traditionally used for such projects, which break down over time. The zone gives pressured fish shelter to reorient before being released back into the main lake, and builds on a similar project completed with a local high school fishing team in 2024.
stories.spoken: Bass caught in tournaments at Oklahoma's Lake Eufaula now have a purpose-built place to recover before being released. Major League Fishing's conservation arm has worked with state wildlife officials to install a network of durable, artificial habitat structures at the mouth of a sheltered cove, replacing the cedar trees once used for the same job, which break down within a few years. The new zone lets stressed fish settle before swimming back into the main lake, and follows on from a similar restoration project the organisation ran with a local high school fishing team two years ago.

stories.headline: New artificial reef off Coffs Harbour to boost fish habitat
stories.summary: A new artificial reef has been installed off Coffs Harbour on the New South Wales mid-north coast, using two steel structures weighing fifty tonnes each, set in twenty-eight metres of water about five hundred metres apart. Funded by the Recreational Fishing Trust and the Marine Estate Management Strategy and built by marine firm SMC Marine, it is designed to attract mackerel, snapper, mulloway and baitfish. It becomes the thirteenth artificial reef deployed along the New South Wales coast.
stories.spoken: There's a new drawcard for anglers on the New South Wales mid-north coast. A new artificial reef has been placed off Coffs Harbour, built from two steel structures, each weighing fifty tonnes, and set in twenty-eight metres of water around five hundred metres apart. Funded by the Recreational Fishing Trust and the Marine Estate Management Strategy, and built by the marine firm S-M-C Marine, it's designed to draw in mackerel, snapper, mulloway and baitfish. It's the thirteenth such reef now placed along the state's coast.

episodes.title: Barefoot Daily News Bulletin — Tuesday 14 July 2026
episodes.intro: Good morning. It's Tuesday the fourteenth of July, and here's the Barefoot Daily News Bulletin — today's good news from fishing and the outdoors, from the Top End and around the world.

stories.headline: Ohio farm-pond angler lands new state record green sunfish
stories.summary: Pioneer, Ohio angler Jeret Stevens set out to catch a feed of fish for dinner but instead landed a state record. Fishing a farm pond near his parents' home with a simple worm-and-bobber rig, the 38-year-old professional chef hooked a green sunfish that certified scales weighed at 1.35 pounds. The Ohio Department of Natural Resources has confirmed it as the new state record, eclipsing a mark that had stood since 2018.
stories.spoken: A trip to catch dinner turned into a state record for Ohio angler Jeret Stevens. Fishing a farm pond near his parents' home with a simple worm and bobber rig, the professional chef hooked a green sunfish that tipped certified scales at one point three five pounds — enough to claim the new Ohio state record, beating a mark that had stood since twenty eighteen.

stories.headline: Z-Man Fishing launches sinking Kingpin and Stuntman soft lures
stories.summary: Tackle maker Z-Man Fishing has introduced a new sinking formulation of its signature ElaZtech material, debuting in two bass lures: the Kingpin soft stickbait and the Stuntman soft jerkbait. Unlike traditional salt-weighted soft plastics, the density is built directly into the polymer, giving a sink rate that stays consistent no matter how much the bait is fished. Both lures are made in the United States and reach retailers this coming autumn, priced at around seven US dollars each.
stories.spoken: Tackle maker Z-Man Fishing has introduced a new sinking version of its ElaZtech soft plastic material, launching in two bass lures — the Kingpin stickbait and the Stuntman jerkbait. Rather than relying on salt to add weight, the density is built into the polymer itself, giving anglers a sink rate that stays consistent no matter how much the bait is fished. Both lures are made in the United States and land at retailers this coming autumn, priced at around seven US dollars each.

stories.headline: Visiting Georgia angler reels in new Idaho state record brown trout
stories.summary: Georgia angler Caroline Langdale was fishing Idaho's iconic South Fork Snake River with guide Ed Emory of South Fork Lodge when she hooked a brown trout that has since been certified as a new Idaho catch-and-release state record. The fish was measured and released unharmed, with Langdale telling Idaho Fish and Game she never expected the fish of a lifetime that day.
stories.spoken: Visiting angler Caroline Langdale, from Georgia, was fishing Idaho's South Fork Snake River with a local guide when she hooked what's since been certified as a new Idaho catch-and-release state record brown trout. The fish was measured and released unharmed, and Langdale said she never dreamed her day on the water would produce the fish of a lifetime.
stories.source: The Daily Caller / Idaho Fish and Game

stories.headline: US states rally anglers to help stop the spread of invasive species
stories.summary: State wildlife agencies across the United States are enlisting recreational anglers in the fight against invasive fish species this month. Wisconsin's two-week Great Lakes Landing Blitz has volunteers greeting boaters at ramps with clean, drain and dry advice, while Colorado and Maryland are running cash-incentive tournaments targeting invasive pike and blue catfish, and Florida's Lionfish Challenge rewards anglers for every lionfish they remove from state waters.
stories.spoken: Wildlife agencies across the United States are calling on recreational anglers to help stop the spread of invasive fish species this month. In Wisconsin, a two-week Great Lakes initiative has volunteers greeting boaters at ramps with simple clean, drain and dry advice, while Colorado and Maryland are running cash-incentive tournaments targeting invasive pike and blue catfish, and Florida's Lionfish Challenge rewards anglers for every lionfish they remove from state waters.

episodes.title: Barefoot Daily News Bulletin — Monday 13 July 2026
episodes.intro: Good morning. It's Monday the thirteenth of July, and this is the Barefoot Daily News Bulletin, bringing you the good news from the water, the bush and the bar-mouth, from the Top End and further afield.

stories.headline: NSW clubs invited to apply for Gone Fishing Weekend support packages
stories.summary: The New South Wales Government is calling on fishing clubs and community organisations to apply for Gone Fishing Weekend support packages, ahead of the national event on Saturday the seventeenth or Sunday the eighteenth of October. Successful applicants receive rod-and-reel sets, goodie bags, circle hooks, fish measuring rulers and recreational fishing resources to help run a community fishing day. Applications close on the eleventh of August twenty twenty-six.
stories.spoken: Now, some good news for community fishing groups. The New South Wales Government is calling for fishing clubs and organisations to apply for Gone Fishing Weekend support packages, ahead of the national event on the seventeenth and eighteenth of October. Successful applicants will receive rod and reel sets, goodie bags, circle hooks and fish measuring rulers to help deliver their own community fishing day. Applications close on the eleventh of August.

stories.headline: Bassmaster unveils 2026 rankings of America's 100 best bass lakes
stories.summary: Bassmaster Magazine has released its 2026 100 Best Bass Lakes rankings, timed to the United States' two-hundred-and-fiftieth anniversary on the second of July. The list showcases the nation's premier largemouth, smallmouth and spotted bass fisheries from coast to coast, with California's Clear Lake topping the rankings ahead of Lake J.B. Thomas in Texas, the St. Lawrence River in New York, the Santee Cooper Lakes in South Carolina and Lake St. Clair in Michigan.
stories.spoken: Over in the United States, Bassmaster Magazine has released its 2026 rankings of America's hundred best bass lakes, timed to the country's two-hundred-and-fiftieth birthday. Clear Lake in California topped the list, ahead of Lake J.B. Thomas in Texas, the St. Lawrence River in New York, the Santee Cooper Lakes in South Carolina and Lake St. Clair in Michigan.

stories.headline: Bureau of Meteorology declares El Niño underway, monsoon onset likely delayed in the north
stories.summary: The Bureau of Meteorology has declared that El Niño is now underway in the tropical Pacific and is likely to persist into next year, with some forecast models pointing to one of the strongest events on record. For northern Australia, including the Top End, El Niño typically means a delayed monsoon onset, below average spring rainfall and warmer than average days, alongside an increased fire risk further south.
stories.spoken: A weather update with real implications for Territory fishing. The Bureau of Meteorology has declared that El Niño is now underway, and it could be one of the strongest events on record. For the Top End, that typically means a delayed monsoon onset and a drier, warmer run through spring, which is worth keeping in mind when planning the next few months on the water.

stories.headline: Garmin unveils LiveScope 2 live-scan sonar series
stories.summary: Garmin has launched its LiveScope 2 series, the next generation of its live-scanning sonar technology, with three new transducer models offering up to 20 per cent higher resolution and a redesigned installation that removes the need for an external black box. The range includes a standard model scanning out to 250 feet, a shorter-range high-detail model, and a cold-weather ice fishing transducer, priced from 1,999 to 2,199 US dollars.
stories.spoken: In fishing technology, Garmin has unveiled its LiveScope 2 series of live-scan sonar. The new units offer up to twenty per cent higher resolution, a simpler installation with no external black box, and models tailored for open water, close-range casting and cold-weather ice fishing. Prices start at around two thousand US dollars.

stories.headline: Georgia's coast sees a hot start to July with red snapper season on hold
stories.summary: Georgia's saltwater fishing report for July describes much warmer than normal coastal water temperatures driving steady inshore fishing for flounder, redfish and trout, with tarpon expected to arrive on the beachfronts through the month. Offshore, the genuine red snapper season remains closed pending a federal court case, while gag grouper fishing stays open until the second of August.
stories.spoken: Over in the United States, Georgia's coast is off to a hot start for July, with warmer than normal water pushing good flounder, redfish and trout fishing inshore, and tarpon expected on the beaches as the month goes on. Offshore, the red snapper season remains on hold pending a federal court case, while gag grouper fishing stays open until early August.

stories.headline: Fly-rod anglers join the dice and urchin lure craze
stories.summary: As soft-plastic dice and urchin lures sweep bass fishing, fly-rodders have begun tying their own scaled-down versions for panfish and bass. Iowa angler Gene Murray makes mini dice bugs from cut-down soft-plastic worms and rubber legs on a size six hook, taking bluegill and largemouth bass to four and a half pounds on a three or four weight rod, while a Washington angler is tying a yarn-based version aimed at largemouth and smallmouth.
stories.spoken: A fresh idea from the fly bench. As dice and urchin lures sweep bass fishing overseas, fly-rod anglers have started tying their own scaled-down versions. One Iowa angler makes mini dice bugs from cut-down soft-plastic worms and rubber legs, taking bluegill and bass up to four and a half pounds on a light fly rod, while a Washington angler is trying a yarn-based version for largemouth and smallmouth.
stories.link_label: ▶ Read how the dice-bug flies are tied

stories.headline: Missouri angler lands a pair of catfish totalling 178 pounds in one hookup
stories.summary: Missouri River regular Brad Hilton hooked two blue catfish at once on cut buffalo fish baits, landing a 77-pounder he released and a 101.36-pound fish measuring 56 inches long. Fishing from an 18-foot aluminium boat on heavy tackle, Hilton said the pair, weighing just over 178 pounds combined, fulfilled a lifelong ambition to land a 100-pound-plus catfish from the Missouri or Mississippi rivers.
stories.spoken: A big one from the Missouri River. Angler Brad Hilton hooked two blue catfish at the same time on cut buffalo fish baits, landing a seventy-seven pounder he released, plus a one-hundred-and-one-pound fish measuring fifty-six inches long. Combined, the pair weighed just over one hundred and seventy-eight pounds, and Hilton said it fulfilled a lifelong ambition to land a hundred-pound catfish.

stories.headline: Michigan shoreline angler lands 48.1-pound flathead catfish
stories.summary: Andrew Karsten was fishing the tailrace below the Berrien Springs Dam on Michigan's St. Joseph River when a dam-gate release triggered a feeding frenzy, and he hooked a flathead catfish on a live rock bass bait. The fish measured 46 inches and weighed 48.1 pounds, and was released after being measured for a Michigan Master Angler catch-and-release entry.
stories.spoken: From the United States, a shoreline catch worth a mention. Angler Andrew Karsten was fishing below a dam on Michigan's St Joseph River when a gate release triggered a feeding frenzy, and he hooked a flathead catfish on a live rock bass bait. The fish measured forty-six inches and weighed forty-eight-point-one pounds, and was released after being logged for a state catch-and-release record.

stories.headline: Florida angler fights off alligator with his own fishing rod
stories.summary: North Fort Myers angler James Grayson McMicken, 71, was attacked by an alligator moments after his first cast on a canal bank, and was dragged into the water before fending it off by jabbing the animal in the eye with his fishing rod. McMicken required stitches and staples for bite wounds but says he plans to return to fishing once he recovers, with wildlife officers still searching for the animal involved.
stories.spoken: A story with a happy ending from Florida. Seventy-one-year-old angler James Grayson McMicken was attacked by an alligator moments after his first cast, and was dragged into a canal before fighting the animal off by jabbing it in the eye with his own fishing rod. He needed stitches and staples for his injuries, but says he is already planning his next fishing trip.

stories.headline: New artificial reef installed off Coffs Harbour to boost fish stocks
stories.summary: Two new steel artificial reef structures have been installed about two kilometres south-west of Sawtell and ten kilometres south of Coffs Harbour, each with a footprint of 15.6 by 15.6 metres, standing 12 metres tall and weighing 50 tonnes. Designed by naval architects, coastal engineers and biologists, the reefs are built to support mackerel, snapper, mulloway and baitfish, and join twelve other artificial reefs already installed along the New South Wales coast. The project was funded through the Recreational Fishing Trust and the Marine Estate Management Strategy.
stories.spoken: Some good news for the conservation and habitat side of things. Two new artificial reefs have been installed off Coffs Harbour, each a fifty tonne steel structure standing twelve metres tall, designed to support mackerel, snapper, mulloway and baitfish. They join twelve other reefs already along the New South Wales coast, funded through the Recreational Fishing Trust and the Marine Estate Management Strategy.

episodes.title: A new reef down south and a barra tug-of-war
episodes.intro: G'day Watty, and good morning — it's Friday the tenth of July, another cracker of a dry-season day in the Top End. Fresh news was a bit thin on the ground this morning, so we're running lean rather than padding it out. But there's a good one out of New South Wales worth knowing, and a fly clip to fire you up for the weekend. Here's your Territory Wrap.

stories.headline: New artificial reef sunk off Coffs to fire up the fishing
stories.summary: A new artificial reef has been installed off Coffs Harbour on the NSW Mid North Coast to boost sustainable recreational fishing and coastal tourism. It's made up of two 50-tonne steel structures, each 15.6 by 15.6 metres and 12 metres high, engineered by naval architects and biologists to hold up in a one-in-100-year storm and placed about 500 metres apart in 28 metres of water, roughly 10km south of Coffs. Designed to draw mackerel, snapper, mulloway and baitfish, it's the latest of 14 offshore reefs now along the NSW coast, funded through the Recreational Fishing Trust and built by SMC Marine.
stories.spoken: First up, a good one out of New South Wales. There's a brand-new artificial reef been sunk off Coffs Harbour, built to boost the rec fishing and a bit of coastal tourism along with it. It's a serious bit of kit — two steel structures, each about fifteen and a half metres square and twelve metres tall, weighing fifty tonnes apiece. They've been dropped around five hundred metres apart in twenty-eight metres of water, about ten kilometres south of Coffs. The whole thing was designed by naval architects and biologists to cop a one-in-a-hundred-year storm and keep sitting there, quietly turning into prime habitat for mackerel, snapper, mulloway and baitfish. It's the fourteenth of these reefs now along the New South Wales coast, paid for out of the Recreational Fishing Trust — so that's your fishing licence money going straight back into more fish. Good to see.

stories.headline: Barra on the fly: a proper tug-of-war worth a re-watch
stories.summary: Flylords' 'Video of the Week' follows Australian angler Mathew Scholz — of the Pelagic Pursuit channel — sight-casting to big barramundi on fly. It's classic tropical stuff: fish tucked hard into heavy structure, big rods, and some genuinely wild footage once the barra decide they've had enough. Not fresh news, but a cracking bit of watching to warm up the casting arm before the weekend.
stories.spoken: And to round it out, a bit of fly inspiration for the drive home. Flylords ran a clip following an Aussie bloke, Mathew Scholz — he's the fella behind the Pelagic Pursuit channel — sight-casting to big barra on the fly. It's the good stuff: fish jammed up tight in heavy structure, big rods, and once he hooks up it turns into an absolute tug-of-war with one of the angriest fish in the country. It's not hot-off-the-press news, but it's a ripper watch to get the casting arm warmed up before the weekend. Link's on the dashboard if you want a squiz.

episodes.title: Snapper smiles and a saltwater double haul
episodes.intro: G'day Watty, and good morning — it's Wednesday the eighth of July, another ripper of a dry-season day in the Top End. Bit of a short wrap today: the fresh news was thin on the ground, so we're running lean rather than padding it out. Two good ones though — a cracker of a comp down south, and a fly tip worth a look on the drive home. Here's your Territory Wrap.

stories.headline: 154 boats, good vibes and a big red: the Trophy Hunter Snapper Bash
stories.summary: The 2026 Trophy Hunter Snapper Bash pulled a field of 154 boats to Coffs Harbour on the NSW Mid North Coast, chasing a prize pool worth more than $200,000. Run by Sondal Bensan and Mike Bonnici as a team-centric successor to the old Dave Irvine Snapper Classic, the comp is known as much for its camaraderie and family-friendly culture as its fishing. Tai Donelan and his crew took out the top prize with a bag of ten cracking snapper.
stories.spoken: First up, a good-news story from down south. The Trophy Hunter Snapper Bash ran out of Coffs Harbour on the New South Wales mid-north coast, and it pulled a huge field — a hundred and fifty-four boats, all chasing a prize pool worth more than two hundred thousand dollars. It's run by a couple of blokes, Sondal Bensan and Mike Bonnici, who took over from the old Dave Irvine Snapper Classic and rebuilt it as a team comp. By all accounts it's less about the cash and more about the good yarns and the camaraderie — phone calls between drifts, a beer at the pub after stop-fishing, families making a week of it. The winners this year, Tai Donelan and his crew, blew the field away with a bag of ten cracking snapper. Sounds like a bloody good week on the water.

stories.headline: Sharpen your saltwater double haul: a quick video refresher
stories.summary: Fly-fishing blog Gink & Gasoline has posted a short video breaking down the double haul — the cast that adds the line speed and distance you need most in the salt. It's aimed squarely at saltwater anglers who find the double haul mystifying, and it's a handy watch for anyone chasing barra, queenies or GTs on fly this dry season.
stories.spoken: And here's one for the fly crew. The blokes at Gink and Gasoline have put up a short video breaking down the double haul — that's the cast that gives you the line speed and the distance, and there's nowhere it matters more than in the salt. A lot of anglers find the double haul a bit of a mystery, so if that's you, it's well worth a watch on the drive home. Get that cast dialled in and you'll be putting the fly where the barra, the queenies and the trevally actually are.

episodes.title: The Territory Wrap — Tuesday 7 July 2026
episodes.intro: G'day Watty, good morning. It's Tuesday the seventh of July, another cracker of a dry-season day in the Top End. Here's your Territory Wrap — the good news from the bush, the boat and the bar-mouth. It's a leaner batch than usual today, the fresh news was a bit quiet, but it's all worth knowing.

stories.headline: Christmas Island still delivering the goods on the flats
stories.summary: A fresh trip report from Kiritimati — the Pacific flats fishery Angling Adventures sends anglers to — describes endless bonefish, hard-won giant trevally and that famously relentless wind. The writer's verdict: it's hunting, not harvesting, and it rewards anglers who back their own skills. Lodges and hosted trips remained busy through 2026.
stories.spoken: First up, one for the travel side of things. A fresh write-up from Christmas Island — Kiritimati, out in the Pacific — paints the usual picture: bonefish everywhere, giant trevally that make you earn every one, and that relentless wind. The angler's take was a good one: it's hunting, not harvesting. You back your own skills and the island rewards you. Still a cracking destination, and the lodges have stayed busy right through the year.

stories.headline: Aussie-made lures and flies star at this month's Expo
stories.summary: The Australian Lure, Fly & Outdoors Expo — a celebration of Aussie-made lures, lure-making and fly-tying — runs 11 to 12 July at Fernvale Showgrounds in Queensland. Expect timber, plastic and metal lures, wet and dry flies, vintage collectables to trade, plus rods, reels and gear. Entry is $15 for adults and free for under-16s with a paying adult.
stories.spoken: A good one for the lure and fly tragics now. The Australian Lure, Fly and Outdoors Expo is on this coming weekend, the eleventh and twelfth of July, at the Fernvale Showgrounds in Queensland. It's a celebration of Aussie-made gear — timber, plastic and metal lures, wet flies, dry flies, even old vintage collectables to trade. Fifteen bucks in for adults, free for the kids under sixteen. Well worth a look if you're that way inclined.

stories.headline: Victoria's next 10-million-fish stocking season kicks off
stories.summary: Victoria's record fish-stocking run rolls into a new season from this July, with the Victorian Fisheries Authority's draft plan to release more than 10 million fish across around 247 waters through to June 2027 — from Murray cod and trout to conservation species like Macquarie perch. More fish in more waters for anglers right across the state.
stories.spoken: Down south, the fish stocking just keeps on coming. Victoria is rolling into a new season from this month, with the Fisheries Authority planning to put more than ten million fish into around two hundred and forty-seven waters between now and June next year. Everything from Murray cod and trout to threatened species like the Macquarie perch. More fish, more waters, more spots to wet a line.

stories.headline: Queensland opens up more dams for stocked fishing
stories.summary: Queensland's Stocked Impoundment Permit Scheme has hit its 25th year and expanded to 66 dams and weirs, with new waters including Paradise Dam (bass and barramundi), Mount Morgan No. 7 Dam and Enoggera Dam now stocked. More stocked impoundments means more places to chase a fish closer to home.
stories.spoken: And staying with more fish in the water — Queensland's stocked-dam scheme has ticked over twenty-five years and grown to sixty-six dams and weirs. New waters coming online include Paradise Dam, now stocked with bass and barramundi, along with Mount Morgan and Enoggera. More stocked impoundments means more places to chase a fish closer to home. A good problem to have.
